Transcrição Completa: Ad Optimization Creative Testing Best Practices
[00:00]
"All right, everybody. Welcome in. So, today we're going to be going through some ad optimization and creative testing best practices. This has come up quite frequently over these last several weeks. So, I wanted to make sure that we dedicated a weekly group call to it."
[00:15]
"The last two weeks of weekly group calls have obviously been occupied by the FTC training and then the Amber operation call. So, we've put this one off for a bit, but here we are."
[00:25]
"So, first of all, let me get started by saying this. A lot of you guys, you guys are what Chad advertisers call nerds. Okay? The way that most of you advertise is so awful."
[00:38]
"Like, you focus on the absolute dumbest shit. And I'm going to completely reform your mental state today to advertise like a Chad. Okay?"
[00:48]
"It's very important that you follow these instructions because they will be highly beneficial to you and enable you to actually scale for the first time in your life."
[01:00]
"Most of the time and most advertisers out there, they get extremely caught up in all of the statistics and data that's available to you. And what they end up doing is split testing their way into poverty or a stagnant level of growth."
[01:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Devon's Campaign Results"
NÚMEROS: $130 CPM, 0.5% CTR, 7x ROAS
VISUAL: Exemplo de campanha com métricas aparentemente ruins mas ROAS excelente, contraste entre estatísticas individuais vs resultado final
[01:18]
"A perfect example of this is we have one of our inner circle members right now. His name's Devin. Devon's ads are absolutely amazing. I get absolutely slaughtered by these ads every single time I log on to one of these platforms."
[01:32]
"Devon's total result so far is about a 7x ROAS. Okay, but here's the thing. Let me read you some statistics real quick from Devon's campaign. He has a $130 CPM. He has a 0.5% click-through rate, link click-through rate, but again, he's at a 7x ROAS."
[01:52]
"Somebody tell me if that's good or bad. Put in the chat. Good or bad."
[01:58]
"Well, most of the nerds that are out there that advertise are like, 'Oh, dude, I got $130 CPM.' Like, I can't scale that. It's like, what the, you know, he has 7x ROAS. Like, who cares you have $130 CPM? It could be $500 if you're printing seven to one. It's a good CPM."
[02:18]
"He has a 0.5% link click-through rate. That's awful. But does it matter when you have a 7x ROAS? Right. What actually matters in that example to do when you have a 7x ROAS? Somebody tell me."
[02:32]
"Yeah. Scale. You scale that shit. You don't just sit on it. You scale it."
[02:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Nerd vs Chad Advertiser Mindset"
NÚMEROS: Benchmarks estáticos vs ROAS dinâmico
VISUAL: Comparação visual entre advertiser "nerd" focado em métricas individuais vs "Chad" focado em ROAS e escala, ícones de cérebro vs músculos
[02:40]
"But I will tell you with as much honesty as I possibly can. There are many of you, okay, sitting on this very call that have had those exact same circumstances present themselves in varying degrees with varying statistics."
[02:55]
"And because you have a benchmark in your head about what a statistic can be, you do not scale extremely profitable or even profitable campaigns. You instead split test your way to poverty."
[03:08]
"You got to understand, you ever heard that quote, 'Strike while the iron's hot.' You guys remember that? That was a blacksmith quote from way back in the day that carried all the way through into the year you and I are alive, pressing buttons and clicking keys on our keyboards."
[03:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Strike While The Iron's Hot"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Metáfora visual de blacksmith forjando metal quente, paralelo com timing de escala em advertising, ícone de martelo vs botão de escala
[03:28]
"Strike while the iron is hot simply means back in the day when you're crafting a sword or some piece of metal. You obviously turned it beat red to be able to use it and transmorph it into whatever kind of tool you wanted to craft it into."
[03:43]
"If you didn't strike it, okay, while it was literally hot, you would fail to obviously do anything with the sword. It's the same thing with advertising when it comes to scaling."
[03:54]
"When we have a set of statistics in front of us that we have profitability with, okay, we want to consider that to be the time frame to start aggressively scaling."
[04:05]
"So when you hear me use the word chadvertise, okay, chadvertising is very simple. It means we want to get the statistics to a point where we are profitable and then we want to focus on scaling."
[04:18]
"And while we're scaling, we want to fix shit and improve shit. But more importantly than anything else, we want to scale."
[04:27]
"That's the number one reason all of you are here is to be able to make an aggressive amount of additional cash above and beyond what you currently make now. Right? How do we get there? Right? What's the simplest one-word answer for how we get there?"
[04:42]
"Scale. Okay? Scale."
[04:46]
"So, without further ado, let me jump into this. Share my screen. I just want to preface it with that. Okay? Because if you look at what I'm about to talk about from a nerd point of view, you'll get lost in the sauce and you won't actually get the takeaways that I'm attempting to make here with you right now."
[05:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Chadvertising Mindset"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Definição visual de "Chad advertising" - foco constante em escala, não em métricas isoladas, postura de confiança vs análise paralisante
[05:08]
"So, for starters, like I said, we want to constantly be in the mindset of Chadvertising. Okay? We are Chads. We are not nerds. We are Chads."
[05:18]
"When we advertise, we are constantly focused on scale. Okay? That is what matters is scaling. Scaling is where we make more money."
[05:28]
"Obviously, when we launch some shit if it's not working as good as what we want it to, we ideally want to get it more profitable, but in the meantime, we want to scale."
[05:38]
"Timothy, unmute yourself. Tell me your ROAS."
[05:42]
"Two."
[05:43]
"Timothy's at a two to one ROAS right now. That means that Timothy is turning a dollar into $2. Okay, Timothy, tell me your CPMs."
[05:52]
"I don't even know them, honestly. It's like 40 bucks maybe."
[05:56]
"Good advertising. Who cares about the statistic when it's 2 to 1? You're printing money."
[06:02]
"If it's a statistic that matters, it's something that we've considered a bottleneck. Do you have any bottlenecks that you've identified, Timothy?"
[06:10]
"Yeah, we basically just need more closers and then ramp up the closers faster so their close rate stays in KPI. That's the biggest bottleneck."
[06:18]
"And so in your advertising and sales statistics, CPMs, link click-through rate, application rate, show rate, close rate, AOV, things like this. Are any of those bottlenecked currently and deemed by you or your advertising staff as something that needs improved?"
[06:35]
"No."
[06:36]
"Fantastic. So, what's that mean?"
[06:39]
"Spend more money and bring on more closers."
[06:42]
"That's right. Now, listen, explain to everybody that you make money not just on the front end, but you also make money every month in perpetuity off the people you're able to retain. Is that correct?"
[06:53]
"Correct. Yeah."
[06:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Front End vs Back End Revenue Model"
NÚMEROS: 2x ROAS front-end, $80K spend = $160K revenue, continuidade mensal
VISUAL: Diagrama de fluxo de receita: inicial 2x + receita recorrente mensal, setas mostrando crescimento perpétuo
[06:57]
"So what Timothy's doing here in this first example is he's turning a dollar into $2 within the first 30 days. Is that correct?"
[07:05]
"Correct. Yep."
[07:06]
"And so every month that is beyond that, you technically accumulate additional cash, right?"
[07:12]
"Yep. Yeah. From continues to grow."
[07:15]
"Yeah. Exactly. Exactly."
[07:17]
"So moral of the story, hey, listen, we're going to get to it. Jesus. Okay. Long story short, we're focused on scaling."
[07:26]
"You see what I mean? Scaling in this example because guess what, Timothy? Tell the people the amount you spend currently."
[07:33]
"Uh we're on pace to spend 80k this month."
[07:37]
"So 80K equals 160K in this example, right?"
[07:41]
"Correct."
[07:42]
"Okay. So that means that there's a net difference here, technically a gross difference of $80,000, right?"
[07:49]
"Yep."
[07:50]
"Now, how much cooler would it be if Timothy spent 500K and made a million dollars, right? How much cooler could that be? That would be a difference of a total of about $420K in that example. That'd make you a lot happier, right?"
[08:05]
"Yeah, most definitely."
[08:07]
"All right, real quick in the chat. How many of you have had a 2 to 1 ROAS before and you haven't scaled it because you decided to nerd out, try to grow the ROAS first instead of just scaling the shit up."
[08:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Front End + Back End Focus"
NÚMEROS: 30-day ROAS vs lifetime value, múltiplos pontos de receita
VISUAL: Balança equilibrada mostrando importância igual de front-end e back-end, múltiplos fluxos de receita convergindo
[08:23]
"Okay, we don't want to do that. We want to scale. Why? Because in this example, it's a perfect initial representation of a very key lesson."
[08:33]
"We have two things that we can focus on when we are advertising: front end and the back end."
[08:40]
"Timothy in this case is profitable on the front end two to one, but every additional month that goes by, he continues to print more money on the back end."
[08:50]
"So, initially, and this is a big lesson, this is why I talk about it first. Okay, all of the statistics that can consistently fluctuate against you. Does everybody agree that advertising stats and like sales stats like show rate, close rate, AOV and things like this, does everybody here agree that they can obviously fluctuate and rarely if ever are they just consistent and static?"
[09:15]
"So if we know going into the game of advertising that all the statistics that are either in our favor or fuck us can fluctuate. Does that mean that my attention should surely be on the front end? No. It means my attention should equally be on the back end. You see what I mean?"
[09:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Cody Sanchez Challenge Funnel Case Study"
NÚMEROS: $292K spend, 1.9x front-end ROAS, $3.4M back-end cash collected, $10K price point, 12% upsell to $35K, ~300 buyers
VISUAL: Breakdown financeiro completo: investimento inicial → resultado front-end → explosão back-end, funil de upsell com percentuais
[09:38]
"Let me give you a good example. So just recently we had the Cody Sanchez challenge funnel, right? And in that challenge we were a total of 1.9x front-end ROAS."
[09:50]
"However, in many different challenges before, we've gone into the challenge losing money. I don't generally set the expectation you'll be profitable going into a challenge. It's not that it's not possible. It's just not as probable from what we've seen."
[10:05]
"However, in this case, we spent about $292,000 and we got that 1.9x ROAS. Okay, pretty good. However, we don't give a fuck about that. We don't give a fuck about that. What do we care about in this example? What we care about is obviously the backend."
[10:25]
"The backend had a $10,000 price point and on that average AOV, we cash collected $3.4 million on just the back end."
[10:35]
"What's that do to my total ROAS in that example? If I spent $292K and between the front end and backend essentially grossed about $4 million. It grows my ROAS tremendously."
[10:50]
"Now, in addition to that, that right there that I just described is what we're going to call the 30-day ROAS. Okay? Because guess what? The quantity of people out of these 10k buyers that eventually, and this is very important to understand, upsell to the $35,000 price point will be about 12%."
[11:10]
"And there was a little over 300 people that purchased at the 10k price. Okay, so that means I'm going to get about 30-ish people that are going to also spend $35,000."
[11:22]
"And surprisingly, it doesn't take that long for them to spend it. Takes about three months on average for that about 12% number to come to fruition and get those people to upsell."
[11:33]
"So that's just free and clear money right there. Again, my initial point I'm trying to make is, and let me make this extremely clear, like I've had clients, one of my favorite ones to point out is a guy named Mark Jennison."
[11:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Mark Jennison Back-End Focus Strategy"
NÚMEROS: Massive ROAS através de back-end optimization
VISUAL: Comparação de advertiser focado apenas em front-end vs advertiser com forte back-end, gráfico de receita total explodindo
[11:50]
"Mark Jennison would do a huge ROAS on a very consistent basis because he wasn't exclusively focused on front end. He was equally as focused on the back end."
[12:02]
"So, we have a client, they're constantly focused on their cost of acquisition on the front end, which is good. That's by no means a bad thing to focus on. I don't want to demonize that by any means, okay?"
[12:15]
"However, they rarely, if ever, focus on the back end. So, what ends up happening is they end up kind of forcing themselves into limiting what they can do on the front end because they have no backend at all."
[12:28]
"So although yes it might seem like it's not exactly an ad optimization or like creative testing best practice, I just want to start this whole call by making sure you understand clearly ROAS is not only impacted by front-end statistics."
[12:43]
"It is equally as impactful to focus on backend as it is to focus on front end."
[12:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Low Ticket to High Ticket Ascension Model"
NÚMEROS: Dan Martell account - $1.6M/month, front-end liquidation strategy
VISUAL: Funil de ascensão: produto barato → setters → closers → high ticket, objetivo de liquidação vs lucro front-end
[12:52]
"It's like a classic low ticket to high ticket ascension model like what I've demonstrated in this challenge. Or as another example, it's like on the Dan Martell account we have, they do about 1.6 mil a month."
[13:05]
"And almost all of it comes from people being sold something cheap and then the setters contacting those people, putting them onto the closers calendars and upselling them on the high ticket thing, right?"
[13:18]
"The whole goal of the advertising process in that example is literally just to try to liquidate the ad spend. It's not even a goal to try to be profitable on the front end."
[13:28]
"We obviously want to. We have a deep desire to be profitable on the front end, but if we're not, we know that we make all the money on the back end."
[13:37]
"So, in some of those examples, I could be under a one ROAS on the front end, but when I factor in backend, I'm like 12 to one ROAS. You see what I'm saying?"
[13:48]
"So again, just to start off, ad optimization and just focusing on all this in general goes well beyond just the advertising statistics at hand."
[13:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "High CPMs - What Do They Mean?"
NÚMEROS: $100-$200 CPM benchmarks mencionados por participantes
VISUAL: Pergunta provocativa sobre CPMs altos, audiência respondendo com números estáticos, preparação para desconstruir mindset
[14:00]
"But let me let me just go through some of the tactical things that we could do as well. So from an ad optimization perspective, let's just start off at the top and we'll kind of work our way down with the statistics here for the flow of our remaining part of the call here."
[14:15]
"What do high CPMs equal? somebody unmute themselves and tell me what does when an ad platform gives you a high CPM, what are they trying to tell you? What are they trying to communicate?"
[14:27]
"Well, it would usually mean like either you're serving a like your ads are being served to a market that costs more to reach or your ads are just shit and they don't want to serve your ads."
[14:38]
"Yeah, that's right. Great mindset. So, to be clear, what determines a high CPM? Go ahead and put in the chat. Put in the chat real quick. What is a high CPM to you? Put a dollar amount that you consider to be a high CPM."
[14:53]
"Let's see some numbers. Dollar figure in the chat. What's a high CPM? $100. $100. $200. $200. $100."
[15:03]
"Okay, so back to my opening statement. So Devin, as an example, is at $130 CPM. He's at a seven to 1 ROAS. Every single one of you that said what a high CPM was, you put a $100 plus. Is that a high CPM if he's at a seven to 1 ROAS?"
[15:22]
"No. It's not. Okay. So, high CPMs there is no static answer. Okay. There's no static answer. Every person who put an exact dollar figure is wrong."
[15:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "No Static Answers in Advertising"
NÚMEROS: CPMs relativos ao ROAS, não absolutos
VISUAL: X grande sobre números estáticos, seta apontando para ROAS como única métrica definitiva, conceito de dinamismo vs estático
[15:38]
"There is no static answer for what a high CPM is. The correct answer is what is the ROAS, Jeremy, that's what I expect you to say."
[15:48]
"When you get asked a question about what is considered high or low for a statistic in the process, what should the first question actually be that you respond with? What is the ROAS?"
[16:00]
"So to be clear, if my ROAS is dog shit and I have according to you guys a $100 to $200 CPM, then yeah, right away we could consider that we have high CPMs."
[16:12]
"But again, CPMs and any other statistic we talk about are not static answers. Okay, they're dynamic answers relative to the performance of the campaign."
[16:24] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Law Breaking Analogy"
NÚMEROS: 94 mph em zona de 55 mph mencionado
VISUAL: Metáfora visual de quebrar leis vs quebrar "regras" de advertising, conceito de conseguir escapar = não há consequência real
[16:26]
"Remember that as an example, it's like if I go out there in the world and I'm consistently breaking laws, okay? I can break as many laws as I want to break if I don't get caught breaking those laws."
[16:38]
"Certain laws have statutes of limitations for how long you can get punished after breaking said law. It's like, as a good example of this, almost every time I get into one of my vehicles, I speed."
[16:50]
"I consistently break speeding laws. Okay, by the way, this is all hypothetical and not admission of guilt. Okay, long story short, I digress."
[17:00]
"The point I'm trying to make is very simple. If I don't get caught, okay, I did not break the law because technically a law is broken when I get punished. That's really when I break the law."
[17:12]
"I could break traditional static answers for what's considered good or bad in advertising, but if I slip by with a phenomenal ROAS, did I actually break that law? Did I actually need to consider that standard static answer to be true? No. No, I don't."
[17:30]
"One time I got a ticket for going 94 miles an hour in a 55 mph zone. I beat the ticket. Does that mean the government gave me permission to speed?"
[17:40]
"Devon right now is getting a seven to one ROAS with $130 CPM. Does that mean that the platform gave him permission to get fucked on CPMs and still perform 7 to 1?"
[17:52]
"You see like there is no static answer. Chad advertising represents that there is no static answer for all this. What triumphs everything else is ROAS. You guys get what I'm saying? Put a one in the chat if you're picking up what I'm putting down. ROAS trumps everything."
[18:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Platform Communication Through Statistics"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Plataformas de ad não podem ligar para você, então comunicam através de performance e estatísticas, ícones de telefone com X, gráficos falando
[18:15]
"To be fair though, let's get clear on some logic. Okay. When we're thinking with like this ad optimization and creative best practices, we obviously have to factor the platform into the equation."
[18:28]
"One thing that I like to consider is, okay, from an efficiency perspective, the ad platforms don't have the bandwidth to be able to pick up the phone and call you and tell you what you're doing right or wrong. They represent that to you with the performance of your campaigns and the statistics."
[18:45]
"So, like Timothy had pointed out, if you do have high CPMs and a poor ROAS, then yes, you can consider that to be you're getting punished for some reason."
[18:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Google Adwords Personal Injury Lawyer Example"
NÚMEROS: $200-$500 por clique, custo extremo mas ROAS justifica
VISUAL: Exemplo de nicho ultra-competitivo, custos astronômicos mas lucros proporcionais, balança custo vs retorno
[18:58]
"You could, and this is very important to understand, there is context here. Okay? If I run Google Adwords campaigns to personal injury lawyers, does anybody want to take a guess of a cost per click for a personal injury lawyer search term that has qualified people?"
[19:15]
"A cost per click. Okay, not a CPM, a cost per click to my website. Somebody guess 150,000, 100 plus, 50, 40."
[19:28]
"Average cost per click, give or take the specific search term, can be upwards of $200 to $500 per click."
[19:38]
"So all the nerds that have static answers for what statistics should be, is that expensive? Go ahead and put in the chat, is that expensive? Yes or no?"
[19:50]
"What's the ROAS? Yeah, now you're getting it. You see, now you're getting it."
[19:56]
"So, long story short, when we look at that, okay, and we are like, how and why are people paying that much cost per click? Well, the answer becomes obvious. There obviously is a huge ROAS on that cost per click."
[20:12]
"And on top of that, for a cost per click to even get that high, that means the competition is extreme. Okay? You see what I'm saying?"
[20:22]
"Versus if you for your $5,000 offer or whatever you're charging, if you had a $500 cost per click for you relative to you, okay, and your ROAS, yeah, sure, that might be considered expensive."
[20:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Click-Through Rate Analysis"
NÚMEROS: 0.5% CTR de Devon, 2% benchmark para "bom" CTR
VISUAL: Comparação CTR ruim com ROAS excelente, benchmark de 2%+ como "fenomenal" em conversion campaigns
[20:40]
"Let's look at click-through rate. So, click-through rate, like again, what's a good click-through rate? Devin, unmute yourself. Tell the people your click-through rate."
[20:50]
"It's like 0.5. It's terrible."
[20:53]
"What's your ROAS?"
[20:55]
"7x right now."
[20:57]
"Okay. Is Devon's 0.5% click-through rate bad? Oh, he's at a seven to 1 ROAS. That's arguably pretty good. You see what I mean?"
[21:08]
"But it's like I will also subscribe to the belief that like a good link clickthrough rate is 2%. Right? Anything above 2% it's phenomenal on a conversion campaign to be clear."
[21:20]
"Anything below that like yeah there's room for performance improvement. But again at a 7x ROAS I'm scaling the fuck out of that thing. I'm not even gonna touch it. I'm doing nothing other than putting more money in, getting more money out."
[21:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "If/Then Scenarios for Optimization"
NÚMEROS: 7x ROAS example maintained
VISUAL: Árvore de decisão: se CPMs altos + CTR baixo + ROAS alto = ESCALAR, oportunidade de teste apenas quando fadiga ocorre
[21:38]
"Now here's the thing. Let me be clear when I say this. I'm going to give you some like if this then that scenarios."
[21:45]
"So, if my CPMs are high and if my link clickthrough rate is low and if my ROAS is high, I'm focused on scaling."
[21:55]
"When my ads fatigue, I'm going to take that opportunity to launch some new tests to try to lower my CPMs and improve my link clickthrough rate."
[22:05]
"Am I going to try to improve my link clickthrough rate or my CPMs in the meantime? Somebody answer yes or no."
[22:12]
"Again, I have high CPMs. I have a low link click-through rate. I'm at a 7x ROAS and to be clear I'm considering changing something. It's like do I change anything?"
[22:24]
"Oh dude, we gas it. Okay, we're gassing it up."
[22:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ad Fatigue - The One Constant"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Conceito de fadiga inevitável, comparado a impostos e morte, única constante garantida em advertising
[22:30]
"And as we scale, okay, what are we inevitably going to always run into as we scale? What happens no matter what in advertising to a campaign? What is the one constant of advertising that we know is inevitable? It's like taxes or death."
[22:48]
"Ad fatigue. Okay. So when this is very important to understand when ad fatigue presents itself those are my opportunity to test."
[23:00]
"You understand why? Well, it's like I got to swap out the creatives anyway. In order to overcome ad fatigue, I have to relaunch the campaign. I have to pull out all the images and videos and I have to put in new ones."
[23:15]
"So guess what? You know when that happens that's my opportunity to test."
[23:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "When To Test - The 3 Opportunities"
NÚMEROS: 3 momentos específicos para teste
VISUAL: Lista numerada: 1) Ad fatigue occurs, 2) ROAS is bad, 3) Start of testing (new campaign), cada um com ícone representativo
[23:22]
"This is very important to understand this right here. What I just described this is what matters a lot is like when is the opportunity to test? when does that opportunity technically exist?"
[23:35]
"Ask yourself the conditions of when opportunities to test are. Okay, so as we just talked about, number one, when ad fatigue occurs. Number two, when ROAS is bad."
[23:48]
"And then number three and this is also important to stand at the start of testing. So, if I launch a new campaign to like a new funnel, a new offer, whatever it is, okay?"
[24:00]
"If I just launch something new, I subscribe personally to anywhere from like a 3-day to sometimes upwards of a 10-day test window before I make any changes to something."
[24:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "3-10 Day Test Window"
NÚMEROS: 3-10 dias, variável baseado em contexto
VISUAL: Linha do tempo de teste, janela flexível de decisão, não estática
[24:14]
"It's a variable answer to be clear. There's not really a lot of static answers you're going to receive out of this. 3 days to 10 days, like I'll give you a great example, right?"
[24:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Brand New Client Case Study"
NÚMEROS: $500/day spend, 3 dias = $1,500, 0 qualified leads inicialmente, $70K liquid capital requirement
VISUAL: Caso detalhado: novo cliente, nova oferta, novo funil, novos ads, performance inicial ruim mas sinais de vida após ajuste
[24:28]
"I had a campaign that we launched for a client. Brand new client, brand new offer, brand new funnel, okay? Brand new ads, everything was brand new."
[24:38]
"In the first three days of the campaign at $500 a day in spend, I got nothing but dog shit leads. Okay, I only had unqualified people."
[24:50]
"My CPMs weren't bad. My link click-through rate wasn't that bad. My even my conversion rate on the page wasn't that bad. It was just a bunch of dog shit leads."
[25:02]
"However, in 3 days, I'd spent $1,500 and I didn't get a single good lead. So, that means that my cost per qualified call was infinite because I hadn't gotten one yet."
[25:15]
"Now, to be clear, I looked at what was off and I determined it was the messaging. So, I went back to the client and I told him, I'm like, 'Hey, man. I need you to refilm all this shit.'"
[25:28]
"I need you to start every single ad with you've got $70,000 liquid or access to $70,000 in working capital. And then I would have him say the rest of the ad hook."
[25:40]
"Every single ad out of 20 that he filmed started off with that exact same thing. I relaunched my campaigns with those new creatives on day four."
[25:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Second Test Results"
NÚMEROS: $200-$280 cost per call range primeiros 3 dias, depois caiu para $95-$105 estável
VISUAL: Gráfico de tendência: custo alto inicial → queda progressiva → estabilização baixa, linha do tempo de 10 dias
[25:52]
"I then again I waited a minimum of three days. Now, within that three days, I had about a $200ish dollar cost per call. It ranged, give or take the day, from like 200 all the way up to like 280."
[26:08]
"It was just bouncing in the 200s for that first 72 hours. Okay, there is no ROAS yet because there's only been like a handful of calls that came in, but I got some signs of life now."
[26:20]
"This is really important to understand. I saw that there was a good like set of leading indicators. Okay, my leading indicators were good. I liked what I was seeing on that second test."
[26:32]
"So, instead of just cutting it off because it had a higher cost per call than what I wanted, I'm going to let it ride. I decided to extend the test window from 3 days to 10 days. Give it an additional 7 days to breathe a little bit."
[26:48]
"The reason I chose to do that for that specific instance was I wanted to see if my cost per call could drop as the pixel got more qualified people back to it."
[27:00]
"Over the course of that 10 days, my cost per call went from about 200 to 280 all the way down to where it bottomed out at. It bottomed out at about $95 cost per call."
[27:15]
"It's been stable there literally ever since. The highest cost per call I get now is about $105."
[27:22]
"So, I took it from a $80 range from 200 to 280 all the way down to 95 to 105. But it took 10 days."
[27:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Signs of Life - Leading Indicators"
NÚMEROS: 10 dias, $5K spend total, <50 calls, $10K produto, 0 vendas ainda
VISUAL: Checklist de sinais de vida: stats trending bem, feedback de vendedores positivo, lead quality melhorando, custo caindo
[27:35]
"Now, here's the other cool part. Within that 10 days, okay, outside of my cost per call dropping. So, let me give you some very clear expectations here, okay, for some signs of life."
[27:48]
"Signs of life are like leading indicators. So first of all like stats trending in the direction you want. Okay, salesperson feedback."
[28:00]
"This was the other thing that came up that was a good sign of life on this specific account. The salespeople had nothing but awesome things to say."
[28:10]
"And by the way, this was ice cold traffic. This was not warm audiences. This was not there wasn't a hammer them strategy. This is just straight up direct response to a call funnel with ice cold traffic."
[28:25]
"The salespeople are saying nothing but really good things, though. The sales people are saying, 'Dude, this audience is super sophisticated. They're financially qualified. They're asking all the right questions.'"
[28:38]
"And within that 10 days, I still didn't see a lick of ROAS yet. I didn't see a single sale. Okay, 10 times 500, I'm at about $5k in spend at this point. I have a $10,000 product."
[28:55]
"But I didn't get that many calls yet. You know, I had under 50 still. But does that mean that campaigns need to get cut off?"
[29:05]
"Somebody tell me based on what I just described, should I cut that campaign off or should I let it ride? No ROAS yet. The statistics are good. I'm getting good signs of life. My cost per call trended down and then technically bottomed out. I'm getting good salesperson feedback."
[29:25]
"What do I do, Daniel? The sales cycle's unknown. It's a brand new offer. Nobody knows the sales cycle yet. We're in uncharted waters here. We're in a forest with a bunch of fog. We have no idea where we are. Who knows? Do I let it ride?"
[29:42]
"Yeah. So, listen. Sometimes you got to put your balls on the table and keep your balls on the table. Okay? You can't just pull them off prematurely. You got to be willing to keep them there for a little bit longer than you're comfortable doing."
[30:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Balls on the Table Philosophy"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Metáfora de risco: manter compromisso com teste mesmo sem ROAS imediato quando sinais de vida são fortes
[30:02]
"It's where a lot of you fuck up and you revert back to being a nerd instead of a chadvertiser. Okay? Chad advertising in this particular case would tell us to let it ride. Why?"
[30:15]
"Because we see great signs of life. Okay, that is always how we judge a campaign before we see the ROAS. We judge it on the signs of life, okay?"
[30:28]
"The statistics trending well, salesperson feedback, okay? Lead quality. You see what I'm saying? And it's relative to every single individual conversion funnel you choose to run, right?"
[30:40]
"If it's a webinar, it's like cost per lead is probably going to be my biggest indication, right? If I use like a challenge funnel and I'm getting people to pay for it or like low ticket to high ticket or something like that."
[30:52]
"Again, ideally, I'm just seeing some good statistics at the beginning."
[31:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Campaign Life Cycle - Birth to Death"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Analogia com ciclo de vida humano: nascimento → crescimento → maturidade → morte, paralelo completo com campaigns
[31:02]
"But how does a campaign typically start? Okay, ask yourself this question. There's a start and there's a finish. Every campaign dies. It is inevitable. What kills a campaign?"
[31:15]
"Fatigue. In most instances, that's the number one causation of death to a campaign."
[31:22]
"But I ask you a simple question. Have you ever actually reflected on how a campaign starts versus ends and how it does in the middle? The life cycle of a campaign, right?"
[31:35]
"Like you know how life cycles work. You can reflect on it for human beings. We start off having some babies, okay? We obviously have a kid. We have a little child. Think about the life cycle of a human being."
[31:50]
"Why is a campaign any different? Why wouldn't you reflect on how a campaign is birthed into existence and how it eventually dies and what it consistently trends at along the way?"
[32:05]
"All everybody on this call is different. Yet obviously there's extreme similarities in every one of us. You all have individual lives, but yet all our lives trend relatively the same."
[32:18]
"We fuck and piss ourselves and are helpless when we're a baby trying to learn things. We go to school, right? Some of us play sports, some of us don't. Some of us go to college, some of us don't. We all enter the workforce. We eventually retire. We hang out, we vacation, right?"
[32:35]
"The places you go, the things you do, the careers you pick, the people you know, it's all relatively the same when you look at it, right?"
[32:45]
"Campaigns are the same. There are commonalities amongst every campaign that exists. And how they start, how they perform in the middle, and how they finish is also all relatively the same. It's a pattern."
[33:00]
"Even though they might have different conversion mechanisms that are attached to them, different audiences, different creatives, they're unique, but they're all the same in how they are throughout their life cycle. Does that make sense?"
[33:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Genetic Outlier Campaigns"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Comparação de campanha "geneticamente superior" que performa imediatamente vs campanha normal, criança jacked vs criança normal
[33:18]
"Ask yourself this. just like a baby. Is a campaign probable to just perform exceptionally well right out the gate, right? When you launch a campaign, is is it just probable to rip right away?"
[33:32]
"I mean, it can, right? Have you ever seen a photo of like an extremely jacked toddler? You guys ever seen a photo like that? You know what I'm talking about?"
[33:42]
"I've seen kids with genetics that are in preschool that look like physically superior beings. Like, you can already tell that that kid has a dramatically higher probability."
[33:55]
"Have you ever seen that video of that little Russian girl out in the forest just beating the shit out of a tree and like she's literally chopping down the tree with her hands while she sits there and punches it? You ever seen that?"
[34:08]
"It's like, dude, that girl's genetically superior. Okay, some campaigns they do actually have a probability to perform really well at the beginning."
[34:18]
"Does that mean every campaign is going to perform really well at the beginning? Right? If you just looked at the super exceptional genetic outliers of human beings that can like do really good math when they're like in preschool and you compared your kid to it, you'd call your kid a fuck up you know?"
[34:35]
"Some of you do that with campaigns. like you've had an occasional campaign or you've heard of an occasional campaign just ripping right out the gate and then you act like that's the standard that's going to happen in every campaign you ever launch. Is that the case?"
[34:52]
"What's more probable to occur in that example? What's the reality of the situation?"
[35:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Campaign Performance Reality Check"
NÚMEROS: 3 dias primeira tentativa = falhou, 3-10 dias segunda tentativa = sucesso, 15 dias para primeira venda, 18 dias total
VISUAL: Timeline realista: falha inicial rápida → ajuste → teste estendido → primeiros resultados, não expectativa de sucesso instantâneo
[35:02]
"It's like what I just gave you as an example for the campaign for the client I launched. Like the first the first days of that campaign, I could judge in three days that it was going nowhere. That thing needed put down."
[35:18]
"I had to take it out back behind the barn and put it to bed. All right? That campaign did not deserve to exist any longer. All right? Three days. I'm judging that thing."
[35:30]
"However, for the new campaign I launched with the new creatives, the first three days really weren't all that great either. I just started to see signs of life."
[35:42]
"And then it took 10 days for it to trend to the point that I needed it to. It took 15 days, just an extra five days to see the first two sales come from it."
[35:55]
"At that point, I was about seven grand invested on the campaigns, including the failed one. and I officially had two sales at 12K a piece."
[36:08]
"So I'm at a little over a 2 to 1 ROAS at that point within 15 days on the new campaign, which would put me at 18 days total if you include the failed campaign."
[36:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Campaign Life Cycle Stages"
NÚMEROS: Baixa probabilidade ROAS início, alta probabilidade meio, morte no final
VISUAL: Gráfico de curva: início baixo (teste) → meio alto (escala) → fim (fadiga), probabilidade de ROAS mapeada
[36:25]
"Here's my point. Campaigns have life cycles as everything does. Okay, they perform kind of like shit at the beginning and you have a really low probability for ROAS."
[36:38]
"Think about it like this. This is as simple as it needs to be for the first and again your sales cycle needs to be factored in whether it's a new offer, an old offer, whether it's you know a new sales team, a new sales team member that's getting those calls or an old sales team member that's already a veteran and really good."
[36:58]
"ROAS probability is at its lowest at the beginning. Okay, it's at its lowest at the beginning, especially if you have more than like a 7 to 14 day sales cycle."
[37:12]
"Answer this question. If there's a low probability for ROAS at the beginning of a campaign, what do we judge it on? What do we judge it on in the beginning?"
[37:22]
"Somebody unmute yourself. Tell me how we judge a campaign in the beginning."
[37:28]
"Ben in the chat says, 'Signs of life.' Griffin says, 'Lead quality.' Yeah, signs of life."
[37:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Predefined Signs of Life"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Checklist obrigatório antes de lançar: definir sinais de vida, janela de teste, budget de risco, não "raw dog" sem plano
[37:38]
"So let me ask you this. It's a very simple question. When you launch a campaign, do you define what the signs of life are going to be? Or do you just raw dog it and let whatever kind of emotional reactive thoughts come into your head determine what you do or do not do with that campaign?"
[37:58]
"Most of you have no plan. Okay? You have no awareness of what the signs of life are when you launch whatever campaign it is. So, you just do all kinds of random ass shit. You just shut it off. You turn it. You just It's like you guys are freaking the fuck out all the time when you do things."
[38:18]
"You know, you start spending a little bit of money. Take your balls off the table right away."
[38:25]
"You have to define in a low probability for ROAS window what the signs of life for that campaign are going to be that you judge it on. That way you can determine confidently if you let it ride, scale it back or shut it off. Does that make sense?"
[38:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ad Optimization Foundation"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Fundação de otimização: awareness de sinais de vida + janela de teste + budget predeterminado = decisões confiantes vs reativas
[38:45]
"So remember from an ad optimization perspective, okay, when we get into a campaign, if I have no awareness at all on what the signs of life of that initial campaign are, I'm exclusively judging it on randomness."
[39:00]
"Like I'm just essentially taking projected statistics that I have in my head that are benchmarks for each specific indicator I'm going to look at."
[39:12]
"Like if I just have a static answer like most of you did for CPMs. If I have a static answer for click-through rate, okay, and if I if I just don't look at other things, if I don't know what the signs of life are, I'm extremely probable to be reactive and just prematurely shut it off."
[39:30]
"I need to also have a testing time frame. Okay, so I have to have a test window. The test window is to be predefined. It is a predefined window for how long you are going to leave your balls on the table before you pull them off."
[39:48]
"Pretty simple. It's a predetermined risk. I am going into the test of a campaign saying I'm willing to gamble this to see if it's good or not. Does that make sense?"
[40:00]
"Drop a one in the chat if it's making sense."
[40:05]
"Yeah, good. I'm going to pause real quick. Does anybody have questions on what I'm saying right now?"
[40:12]
"Yeah, Jeremy, I got one. When it comes to those those predefined signs of life, I showed up 15 minutes late, so if I missed it, just let me know. What what are those typically that you're looking for? Those predefined signs of life. And I'm sure it depends on the funnel, but like a call funnel."
[40:30]
"What do the words say at the top of the screen there underneath the signs of life column?"

[40:45]

[41:10]
