[41:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Recent Webinar Campaign Example"
NÚMEROS: $130K budget, $11 cost per lead histórico, $14 inicial (alto), $13 começou vs $8-$11 normal, finish projetado $20
VISUAL: Caso de webinar com tema mudado, opt-in rate baixo causando CPL alto, projeção de piora, necessidade de ajuste rápido
[41:30]
"Like as an example, on this most recent webinar I did, we had $130,000 budget for a client. Historically, every month we've ran that webinar, we get an average of about, okay, an $11 cost per lead at the beginning of the campaign."
[41:48]
"This particular campaign I launched this month with that $130,000 budget, my cost per lead immediately was $14."
[42:00]
"on all the historical performance of these webinar campaigns, we usually start around like as low as eight, maybe as high as 10 to 11. So, right away, me starting off at 13, what's that indicate to me just right off the bat?"
[42:18]
"I have historical data from that account in this case that I can factor in that indicates to me that that's a high cost per lead."
[42:28]
"This particular client for this webinar that we're referring to, they changed the theme of the webinar. So, right away I'm like, 'All right, well, what is making my cost per lead higher? Is it a higher CPM, a lower link click-through rate, a lower optin, or something else?'"
[42:48]
"And it was actually a lower opt-in rate. The lower opt-in rate of the page was actually driving up my cost per lead right off the bat."
[43:00]
"I could also immediately project onto the campaign that I was going to have a really bad time with where my cost per lead was probable to finish. Usually my cost per lead finishes off and this is kind of unfortunate at around like a $12 cost per lead."
[43:18]
"But keep in mind I started that webinar campaign at 13. I right away I was like, 'Dude, I'm probable to finish off at like 20 at this current pace.'"
[43:30]
"So I had to go back to the financial model immediately and assess what I was going to do. I had to start assessing because I was on a timetable for that specific campaign."
[43:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Timetables and Test Windows"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Diferença entre campanhas com deadline (webinar) vs sem deadline (call funnel), impacto na velocidade de decisão e janela de teste
[43:45]
"Some campaigns have timetables like a webinar campaign. There's obviously a date of the webinar coming up. It's a hard date. I got to work around that with speed. That alone can determine a tighter test window."
[44:00]
"That can cut down the amount of time that I'm willing to tolerate something to be on a shorter cycle. Okay. In addition to that, it's going to determine how fast I judge it."
[44:12]
"Moral of the story is on some campaigns like a call funnel as an example. Yeah, like you might deterministically want to make a certain number off of that campaign, but at least there's no hard date."
[44:25]
"So you can be a little more loose with the timetables that you're going to test to accumulate a little bit more data. And also it typically is more expensive to get a cost per call obviously compared to a webinar lead. Does it make sense?"
[44:40]
"Other questions so far. We got a lot more to cover, so ask your questions if you got anything. If not, we're moving on."
[44:50]
"All right, great. Axel has a question. How do you make sure ROAS doesn't go lower as you 5x the spend? Because usually it does. Great question, Axel. We'll talk about that."
[45:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Campaign Life Cycle - Middle Phase"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Curva de performance: início baixo → meio ALTO (máxima probabilidade ROAS) → fim declínio, zona de escala identificada
[45:08]
"So moral of the story. So in the middle of a campaign, right, my probability for ROAS increases dramatically like throughout a majority of the campaign all the way until the finish. This is where my ROAS has the most potential. Simple enough, right?"
[45:28]
"I want to make sure that after my test window has passed that as data accumulates I see what I want to see which is either a few things."
[45:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Bottoming Out Concept"
NÚMEROS: $200-$280 range → $95 estável, $130 = sinal de fadiga
VISUAL: Conceito de "bottoming out" - estatística estabiliza em número consistente, inflação indica fadiga começando
[45:42]
"Number one a bottoming out of the stat. So as I talked about in that call funnel I went from 200 to 280 all the way down to 95. It didn't go lower it didn't go higher. That's called bottoming out."
[45:58]
"It's really important. I want to emphasize this. It's really important to determine the bottomed out number because that's how I can gauge ad fatigue."
[46:10]
"If I know that I have a $95 cost per call and all of a sudden after a few days I see it inflate to $130, although yes, I might still be profitable. The $130 cost per call in that example when when it has averaged and bottomed out at 95 indicates I'm probable to be dealing with ad fatigue. You see?"
[46:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Data Analysis Windows"
NÚMEROS: 1 dia (check only), 3 dias (decisões às vezes), 7 dias (confirmação forte), mês completo (conclusão final)
VISUAL: Três janelas de análise em camadas: diária superficial, 3-dias tático, 7-dias estratégico, month-to-date definitivo
[46:38]
"While my ROAS has the most potential during that like early on middle phase to the end phase of the life cycle of a campaign, my statistics, okay, give or take the day, I might see daily fluctuations. So I have to have a average window that I judge."
[46:58]
"My average judgment window has to be predetermined as well. Okay, although yes, here's typically three windows I like to look through. Okay, number one, seven days, three days, and daily."
[47:15]
"I don't ever really do anything based on the daily. I just check it. The 3-day shows me an average for a tighter time frame. I will sometimes make decisions based on the 3-day trend of data."
[47:30]
"As an example, let's say a campaign's been running all of March up to this point, from the 1st to the 22nd, the day that we filmed this right now. I'll go from the 1st to the 3rd, the 3rd to the 6th, 7th to the 10th, etc."
[47:48]
"I'll look at like little three-day trends. I'll try to find patterns in the data. I'll also look at 7-day trends. So, I'll see what the first week performance was like, the second week performance, the third week."
[48:05]
"I'll see if there's fluctuations between them. I will also look at month-to-date. Month-to-date is one of my favorite views to look at data through."
[48:18]
"So, I'm just going to reiterate that one day I do not do anything with, but I'll check it. Okay? 3 days typically give me a strong indication of where I'm trending. Seven days gives me an even stronger confirmation bias of how I'm trending."
[48:38]
"And the full month to date helps me broaden the time frame to ultimately conclude to what I need to do next."
[48:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Campaign Death and Rebirth Cycle"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Ciclo de morte: ad fatigue → pull old creatives → insert new → publish → kill old campaign → repeat, processo circular contínuo
[48:50]
"Here's the thing. At the finish of a campaign, it can die from all kinds of reasons, but again, the main reason is typically ad fatigue."
[49:00]
"And all we have to do, we just again, we have to relaunch the campaign, pull out all the images and videos, put new images and videos in place, and publish it. Once approved, we're killing the dead campaign."
[49:18]
"We're letting a new one ride, and we're repeating this cycle all over again."
[49:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Campaign Performance Graph"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Gráfico visual detalhado: início (pequeno) = baixa probabilidade → meio (GRANDE) = alta probabilidade + escala → fim (pequeno) = plateau e morte
[49:28]
"It's also important to note that generally during the middle of the campaign, this is when I have the most scaling potential. Okay, this is when I can really let it rip."
[49:40]
"A campaign's performance on a graph will typically look like this. Okay, this specific window would represent the beginning. This specific window would represent the middle and this specific window would represent the finish."
[50:00]
"Again, this tiny little window here is when I am the least probable to have result potential. It's just starting off. It's my test window. Lowest probability for ROAS."
[50:15]
"This big window is when I should be seeing ROAS, when I should be scaling until I get to that eventual plateau. Once I get to the eventual plateau, the inevitable is upon me. I will experience death soon in that campaign and I must be prepared."
[50:35]
"I have to get my ads created. I have to have my surplus of images and videos ready to redeploy."
[50:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Scaling at Plateau = Death"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Warning visual: escalar no plateau = rodar rodas = custo mais alto sem mais resultados = necessidade de relaunch
[50:45]
"If I scale it or I attempt to scale it at the plateau, I will spin the wheels. Okay, I will not experience more results. I will only experience a higher cost per result."
[51:00]
"Therefore, I need to relaunch my campaign and I need to repeat this cycle again. This cycle of time is not a static period of time."
[51:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Campaign Lifespan Variables"
NÚMEROS: $35K-$50K/day = 5-7 dias fadiga, $1K-$5K/day = 3-9 semanas fadiga
VISUAL: Comparação de lifespan: alto budget = vida curta (inseto) vs baixo budget = vida longa, budget determina velocidade de fadiga
[51:15]
"on a campaign that I've recently spent about $35,000 to $50,000 a day on. I was fatiguing in about 5 to seven days."
[51:28]
"The life cycle of that campaign was that of a small insect. It would die rapidly."
[51:35]
"However, if I have a campaign that's at like $1,000 to $5,000 a day, that could last for three to nine weeks before it fatigues."
[51:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "External Variables - Inflationary Events"
NÚMEROS: Fevereiro início, St. Patrick's Day, Spring Break mencionados
VISUAL: Calendário de eventos que causam inflação: Super Bowl, Valentine's Day, President's Day, St. Patrick's, Spring Break = fadiga acelerada
[51:50]
"There are also variables that determine what I need to do beyond my control. Who here experienced a little bit of inflation through the first part of February or potentially leading up to St. Patrick's Day with spring break?"
[52:08]
"Who here experienced inflation? Meaning whatever the normal performance of your campaign was, it went well above that due to events out of your control like the Super Bowl, Valentine's Day, President's Day, and most recently St. Patrick's Day, and Spring Break."
[52:28]
"Those events have heightened levels of spend from advertisers. Although my campaigns might have historically fatigued, let's say an average of five to nine weeks, during inflationary events, they will fatigue rapidly."
[52:45]
"And I have to be prepared to overcome the fatigue far sooner."
[52:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Everything is Dynamic"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Conceito central: TUDO é dinâmico em advertising, não estático, única constante = mudança, preparação para todos cenários
[52:52]
"Remember, I want you to ingrain this word into your head for how you behave as an advertiser. We have to consider that everything is dynamic."
[53:05]
"There is no real static answer for almost anything in advertising. Okay, it is all a dynamic number. There's a dynamic amount of time. Every single thing that we face changes."
[53:20]
"Change is one of the only constants in advertising that exists. So although yes it might feel better to obviously have some consistency and predictability that is rarely the case in advertising. We must be prepared for all scenarios."
[53:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Life Cycle Understanding = Problem Prevention"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Mapa de problemas comuns: lançar e entrar em pânico sem ROAS, tentar iterar quando deveria escalar, fazer coisa errada no momento errado
[53:42]
"So to be clear this is very important to understand the life cycle of a campaign is not a static answer. It is a dynamic answer. Okay. I have to be equipped for when to do what and how to behave at the different stages of a campaign."
[54:00]
"Most of your problems that you bring to me relate to this lack of understanding. You will launch a campaign and be freaking the fuck out that it doesn't have ROAS right away."
[54:15]
"Meanwhile, you have the lowest probability for ROAS when you launch a campaign. I will ask you what are the signs of life you're looking for? What's your predetermined test window? What's your gambling budget for it? How much are you willing to risk? You have none of these answers. That's why you freak out."
[54:35]
"Some of you get to the point where you've gotten a campaign rolling and you need to start the scaling process, but you don't. You instead try to iterate and test while a campaign needs to be stable and scaled."
[54:50]
"You do the wrong thing at the wrong time, you get the wrong result. You do the right thing at the right time, get the right result."
[55:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "McLaren 750S Tunnel Story"
NÚMEROS: 130 mph, 90+ mph, velocidades extremas
VISUAL: Analogia de dirigir rápido: não frear na curva = sucesso, frear na curva = quase desastre, timing correto de ações crítico
[55:02]
"Recently, I've been driving my new 750S Spider through this tunnel that takes you from this bridge that goes to South Beach to the port of Miami. It's the only tunnel I've got access to and it's sweet. It's like a movie."
[55:20]
"Every time I go in there, I rip the car up, this is a hypothetical story and not real to 130 miles an hour. Okay, I'm absolutely punching it when I go through that thing. And there's two different turns in the tunnel."
[55:38]
"Recently, one of my friends who's rich that has a great car, too, went to the tunnel with me and he decided to break during the turn while he was going like 90 something miles an hour."
[55:52]
"For those of you who don't drive cars fast, one of the number one rules of cars is when you're driving fast around a turn, you do not break. It can cause catastrophic failure."
[56:08]
"It changes everything when you break around a turn. You're just supposed to let off the gas if you want to slow down a little bit around a turn. You can't break. Too many things can go wrong. And they almost did for him."
[56:25]
"He almost ran his car straight into a wall because he's dumb and he didn't understand what to do and when."
[56:35]
"You do the wrong thing at the wrong time, you get the wrong result. Okay, I come around the turn absolutely flying. I hold that shit for dear life and I get a great outcome."
[56:50]
"My car rewards me with a nice crackle pop and a loud bang as I downshift into the straightaway and punch it. Okay. Euphoria flowing through me. It's amazing."
[57:05]
"I get the right result at the right time because I did the right thing in the right moment. You see, advertising is the same."
[57:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Life Cycle Awareness Required"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Problema de falta de awareness: entrar em pânico no final sem reconhecer que é hora de "funeral" da campanha, attachment excessivo
[57:18]
"You have to consider the life cycle at the end of a campaign. Some of you are like, 'Dude, my campaign's not performing.' Like, you just start freaking the fuck out for no reason because you're oblivious to the time frame that you're in."
[57:35]
"You're just like, 'Oh, dude, my shit's not performing anymore. Like, what the fuck do I do?' It's like, dog, you give it a funeral, you kill it, okay? Take it out back behind the barn, put it to bed."
[57:50]
"But it's like, oh, dude, this campaign's done so well for me. Yeah, who cares, dog? You're too attached to things."
[58:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "ATTACHMENT - The Killer"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Conceito crítico: attachment a campanhas = morte de performance, lobster vs inseto lifespan, attachment previne letting go
[58:02]
"This is one of the most critical things for you to understand about the death of a campaign. This fucks you more than almost anything else. Attachment."
[58:15]
"If we know that a campaign can have the life cycle of an insect or it can have the life cycle of a lobster, for all those who don't know, lobsters can theoretically live forever."
[58:28]
"To be clear, think about this. If I get attached to it, but I know that it's going to die, I'm not going to be willing to let it go when it has to be put down."
[58:42]
"And again, you're just going to ride it into the ground. Your ROAS is going to get fucked."
[58:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Fired Advertiser Case Study"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Caso real: advertiser demitido por attachment excessivo, nunca superou fadiga corretamente, manteve creatives velhos em novas campanhas
[58:52]
"I have an advertiser on an account that we've been a consultant on. The guy just got fired. He just got let go. Why? It's a pretty simple reason."
[59:05]
"The guy was far too attached to campaigns that are underperforming and he would never actually overcome ad fatigue the way that he needs to."
[59:18]
"Instead, he would stay attached to creatives and try to keep them in every future campaign he'd relaunch with. and he would never get the performance that he should have gotten because he was attached."
[59:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Detachment Mastery"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Conceito de mastery: desapego total = peak results, recriar não reusar, ads devem ser NOVOS sempre
[59:38]
"You have to have detachment mastery to truly have peak advertising results. If you love a specific ad that you've created, recreate it. Do not attempt to run the same one again. It has to be new."
[59:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Social Media Content Philosophy"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Pergunta provocativa: você já viu o MESMO conteúdo ao logar em plataforma? Não = plataformas odeiam repetição
[60:00]
"We've talked about this before. I'll give it to you again here. Now, if you log on to Facebook, Instagram, Tik Tok, LinkedIn, YouTube, tell me a singular time that you've seen the same piece of content."
[60:15]
"When you log onto the platform and start scrolling, tell me a single time that you've seen the same exact video, the same exact image upon logging in."
[60:28]
"What would your experience be like if that was the case? If every time you logged on it was the exact same shit would you go back? Would you use the platforms as much as you do? No."
[60:42]
"What does that indicate the platforms want from us as advertisers? Then they want new creatives."
[60:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Why Ad Fatigue Exists"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Razão fundamental: plataformas odeiam ads repetidos, não podem ligar para você, então punem estatisticamente até você entender
[60:52]
"Why does ad fatigue occur so often? It is one of the longest-running commonalities of advertising because they hate when an ad that you are attached to continues to run and they can't pick up the phone and call you."
[61:10]
"So, they punish you statistically until you finally get it instead. And then magically once you detach from those old creatives, you relaunch the campaign with new ones. The cost per result goes right back down to what it was pre-fatigue and you start ripping great results again."
[61:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Sin of Attachment"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Attachment = pecado mortal de advertising, previne euphoria de chadvertising, punição consistente por práticas de attachment
[61:32]
"Those who practice attachment as advertisers get punished consistently. They never experience the euphoria of chadvertising because they practice the sin of attachment. You understand?"
[61:48]
"Drop a one in the chat if you understand that."
[61:52]
"Oh yeah, dude. I got such great creatives. I just keep running them forever. Fuck you. You have to vary them. They have to change. They must change. You must do new things."
[62:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "How to Make It New"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Táticas de variação: mudar camiseta, local, adicionar chapéu/óculos, mudar B-roll, remover B-roll, dizer mesmas palavras mas fazer NOVO
[62:10]
"You can reiterate and make new what has historically worked with subtle variations. Change the t-shirt. Change the location and say the same words. Change wear a hat, wear glasses, you know, just do the exact same thing, but make it new."
[62:30]
"Add B-roll to it. Change the B-roll in it. Remove the B-roll. Okay? Make it new."
[62:38]
"You can attach yourself to what works, but it still must be new in the eyes of the platform to overcome the fatigue."
[62:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Self-Awareness in Campaign Cycle"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Problema raiz: falta de awareness de onde está no ciclo + falta de plano = pânico e decisões ruins
[62:50]
"So when you guys again, you freak out because you don't recognize with self-awareness where you're at in the cycle of a campaign and you don't have a plan relative to where you're at within the campaign. You see what I mean?"
[63:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Creative Testing Best Practices"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Início de seção tática sobre creative testing, transição de filosofia para prática
[63:10]
"So let's go through some best practices from a creative standpoint. Okay. So, creative testing is another incredible benefit but also a sin depending on how you approach it."
[63:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Surplus Philosophy"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Erro comum: criar e testar imediatamente tudo = ridículo, correto = criar surplus e manter banco de recursos
[63:28]
"One of the number one things I see is people creating stuff and then thinking they need to immediately test all of it. Absolutely silly. A ridiculous belief. You guys are freaks who do that."
[63:45]
"You don't want to do that. I want to continue creating new ads and then have them in like a surplus."
[63:55]
"You guys got to understand the accumulation of resources is sometimes a greater benefactor to you compared to deploying the resources."
[64:08]
"I want to put the players into the game, but I also still need a bench of players for when one of my current players out there in the field gets injured. I got to have a deep roster."
[64:22]
"Otherwise, when one of my people on the field gets injured, what's going to happen then?"
[64:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Budget Determines Testing Variables"
NÚMEROS: $1,000 ou menos = 3-5 imagens/vídeos, 2-3 headlines, 2-3 body copy
VISUAL: Tabela de budget vs variáveis: baixo budget = poucas variáveis, regra fundamental de testing
[64:32]
"So, here's the thing. budget determines the testing variables. Okay? So, let me give you an answer. If I'm at about $1,000 or less, I'm going to do about three to five images or videos."
[64:48]
"I'm going to do about two to three headlines and I'm going to do about two to three different pieces of body copy, otherwise known as primary text."
[65:00]
"Why doesn't matter whether it's dynamic ads or static ads? Why determine this amount of variables to be the most appropriate?"
[65:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Algorithm Overwhelm Problem"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Problema: muito pouco budget + muitas variáveis = algoritmo não consegue gastar efetivamente para determinar o que funciona
[65:12]
"Very simple. I need to make sure that I do not overwhelm the algorithm and the platform with too many variables. Otherwise, it will not be able to spend through them effectively to determine what actually works."
[65:30]
"If I have too little budget and too many variables, I will have a bad time. Yes. This is per campaign, Timothy. Yes."
[65:42]
"If I have too little budget and too many variables, the platform will not be able to efficiently spend through them to determine which set of variables or combination of variables is the best."
[66:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "CBO Campaign Mistake Example"
NÚMEROS: 10 adsets diferentes, 5 ads cada, $500/dia budget = desastre
VISUAL: Exemplo de erro clássico: CBO com muitos adsets + muitos ads + budget baixo = distribuição desigual e reclamações
[66:02]
"Give you a great example of this. Sometimes you'll launch like a CBO campaign, right? And let's say you put 10 different adsets in there with different audiences and on top of that you put like five different ads in there for every individual campaign and adset, okay?"
[66:22]
"but you have like a $500 a day budget and then you're gonna sit there and whine and complain when all of a sudden only one adset gets all the reach and you barely got any reach."
[66:35]
"Like you spent like dollars on the other adsets and some of your ads aren't getting any reach and you're going to be a little whiny boy and you're going to be like, 'Oh, dude, it's not getting any reach. Like CBO sucks.'"
[66:50]
"No, you suck and you're oblivious to the platform's rules and how it works. You can't blame a trillion dollar company when you're talking to me from an apartment or a home."
[67:08]
"You must also have some serious budget to make hard conclusions that it's truly their fault. Almost everything is your fault when it fails to do what you'd otherwise expect it to do."
[67:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "High Budget Variable Example"
NÚMEROS: $10K/day budget = 5 adsets, max variáveis (10 vídeos, 5 headlines, 5 body copy) = 200+ combinações possíveis
VISUAL: Contraste: alto budget permite máximo de variáveis com distribuição relativamente igual inicial
[67:28]
"If I go launch a campaign that has like a $10,000 a day budget, okay, and I have five adsets within it and I'm using dynamic creative within it or static ads and I load it up with the maximum amount of variables, 10 videos, five headlines, five different pieces of body copy."
[67:50]
"Yeah, that's over 200 different combinations of ads that can be made with those variables. At a 10K budget, I will see a relatively equal distribution amongst all of those adsets initially before it starts to bias towards one or the other."
[68:12]
"I will see a relatively equal distribution of my advertising creatives until I start to see a heavy bias towards one specific combination that's worked the best."
[68:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Platform's Helpful Bias"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Plataforma tentando AJUDAR: com muitas variáveis + pouco budget, imediatamente direciona para combinação mais provável de funcionar
[68:30]
"So, if I launch a campaign with too many variables and too little budget, I'm not going to have equal distribution. I'm going to have a heavily biased distribution because the platform's not even going to try to waste your budget."
[68:48]
"It's just going to conclude. It almost does like a scan of like, all right, this specific ad set and this specific ad variables that this advertiser has given us is what we determined to be the most probable to work."
[69:08]
"We're not even going to try to distribute the other budget because it dramatically lowers the probability of this advertiser getting a result."
[69:20]
"we're just going to immediately bias towards one specific outcome and lean into that to try to get this advertiser what they're optimizing for."
[69:32]
"And then you get pissed at the platform and in reality they made that decision to try to help you because you don't understand how to advertise. You see what I mean?"
[69:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Trillion Dollar Company Wants You to Win"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Conceito fundamental: plataforma QUER seu sucesso para você continuar gastando, decisões são para AJUDAR
[69:48]
"So it's like remember the trillion dollar company, they want to help you win, okay? They want you to make money so you continue to give it more budget."
[70:00]
"This is very important to understand. When I spend enough, I can load it up with more variables. But if I load it up with too many variables, the easiest way to tell is upon launching the campaign, it immediately bias towards one thing over the other. And I did not get relatively equal reach."
[70:20]
"Does that make sense to everybody?"
[70:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A - Timothy's Questions"
NÚMEROS: $250/day starting budgets mencionado, múltiplas campanhas
VISUAL: Perguntas sobre budget inicial e account simplification best practices
[70:28]
"We got some questions in the chat I'd like to answer. Timothy says, 'How do you determine what the starting budget should be for a campaign? I'm doing 250 a day for starting budgets currently, but I have tons of campaigns.'"
[70:45]
"Yeah. So, to be clear, I like to first of all practice account simplification best practices. I'm going to try to have as few campaigns as possible for all of the different funnels that we're running."
[71:00]
"I'm going to try to condense them because that's a best practice of the platforms that they prefer. That way, I can concentrate my budget to test more variables within that campaign."
[71:15]
"In addition to that, when I'm starting off a campaign, I'm just determining what my test budget's going to be and what I'm willing to risk, aka what I'm willing to lose."
[71:28]
"So, if that's $250 a day, that's totally fine. That just means I'm going to include less variables on the creative testing side of things. That's all that means. That's okay. I could start off with a $100 a day budget."
[71:45]
"Again, I'm just going to have far less variables that I'm going to test with. You see, remember this, okay? The bigger the budget, the more I can test. The lower the budget, the less I can test."
[72:00]
"I have to concentrate my budget. And the platform's going to do this anyway. That's the whole point of what I just told you is even if you load it up with a bunch of variables."
[72:12]
"It's going to tell you to get fucked and it's going to bias your budget towards a concentrated thing that it deems most probable to convert for you."
[72:25]
"So, you might as well do that anyway if you have a lower budget. Reduce the variables with a smaller budget. That's all I mean."
[72:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A - James's 200 Variations Question"
NÚMEROS: 10 imagens/vídeos × 5 headlines × 5 body copy = 250 combinações totais
VISUAL: Problema matemático de combinações: múltiplos de variáveis criando centenas de possibilidades únicas
[72:38]
"James's question, he says,'Explain how you can efficiently record content in one setting that will get you 200 variations. Is that for an example? You say 12 hooks, two to three bodies, two to three CTAs.'"
[72:55]
"So, James, the 200 variables is determined by that common math problem you did back in school, back in grade school."
[73:08]
"So the variable max I can have 10 images or videos combination or I can have five headlines and I can have five pieces of body copy."
[73:22]
"So long story short when I say this is that problem in grade school maybe you remember it. It was okay. You got three pairs of shoes, got two pairs of pants, you got five t-shirts and a hat. How many combination of outfits do you have with that quantity of clothing material?"
[73:42]
"So in this case, this specific set of 200 combinations is the 10 different images or videos times the five different headlines times the five different pieces of body copy, which actually equates to about 250 total individual variations that can be created out of those individual variables."
[74:05]
"And again, the platform when you load it up with these specific assets, these variables here, images, videos, headlines, and body copy, it will combine them in different combinations to have up to 250 different individual variations in this case. Okay, that's all that math problem is."
[74:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Brandon Carter Filming Efficiency Example"
NÚMEROS: 5 tank tops, 10 locações diferentes, 3-4 horas = dezenas de vídeos
VISUAL: Sistema eficiente: mesma fala + múltiplas locações + múltiplas roupas + múltiplos hooks = volume massivo de content
[74:28]
"Now, moral of the story, it's also very important to point out for your question specifically, if you're in one specific location, right, and you're filming ads, most of the time, like here's probably the most efficient person I've surprisingly ever filmed with is my buddy Brandon Carter."
[74:48]
"So, Brandon would come over at the time to the farmhouse and he would bring with him a small suitcase or bag. He would have about five different tank tops in it because that was Brandon's preferred outfit."
[75:05]
"We had a total of about 10 different places we could film at the farm mansion. We had the pool in the backyard with a few different angles. We had this like little tiki hut thing in the back. We had the cars in the front. We had the house in the front."
[75:25]
"You know, we had a few different angles, simply put, that we could shoot. So what we would generally do is we would shoot the same ad in all those different locations in one shirt and then he would go back and shoot the same ad in a different shirt in the same location"
[75:45]
"and then the same ad in the same locations with another different shirt, you know, and then we'd go back and we would film as an example while he's wearing the same clothes. We would film like two or three different hooks, you know, per location."
[76:05]
"So it's like we would have a total out of that in about a 3 to four hour window of filming of dozens of videos that were filmed, you know, just through a combination of saying the same things in different locations, saying different things in the same locations"
[76:25]
"and then saying the same things in the same locations and different locations in different outfits. That's the moral of the story."
[76:35]
"You just want to bring different clothes with you and have a place that has different locations and just have a few ad scripts. You just kind of go through them one at a time. up to you for how you flow through it."
[76:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Copy/Headline Impact Question"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Hierarquia de atenção: 1) vídeo/imagem PRIMEIRO, 2) headline SEGUNDO, 3) body copy TERCEIRO
[76:52]
"How much does the copy/headline affect their performance? Yeah. So, generally when you have an ad, what you must consider is where people look first."
[77:08]
"Usually how it works is people will pay attention to the video or the image first. They will then pay attention to the headline second and they will pay attention to the body copy third. So in combination it looks like that."
[77:28]
"So headlines matter, body copy matters, but when it comes to overcoming ad fatigue, swapping out headlines and body copy is meaningless. It does absolutely fuck all for overcoming ad fatigue."
[77:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What Actually Overcomes Fatigue"
NÚMEROS: Anos rodando mesmo copy mencionado
VISUAL: Verdade fundamental: apenas trocar imagens/vídeos supera fadiga, headlines e body copy são secundários
[77:48]
"Swapping in new images or videos is always what overcomes ad fatigue. We have accounts that we've ran the same copy on for without exaggeration years. We just consistently swap in and out new images and videos."
[78:08]
"Again, I am happy to be clear to continue testing new body copy and new headlines. I'm not trying to make it sound like I'm not I'm just trying to emphasize my point for the weight that images and videos hold in the process."
[78:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Targeting and Machine Learning Variables"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Lista completa de fatores que impactam targeting: vídeo/imagem, headline, body copy, lead form, landing page, vídeos na página, evento otimizado
[78:30]
"How much do headlines and body copy affect targeting and machine learning? So there are several variables that impact targeting and it goes well beyond just the body copy and the headlines. It's everything."
[78:48]
"So what's said in the video or the image, the headline, the body copy, the lead form, the landing page, the videos on the landing pages, all of that is very strong in terms of the bias for who it targets."
[79:08]
"Combined with the standard event that you are optimizing the campaigns for, that matters a whole lot. It is all factored in."
[79:20]
"That's why as an example, broad targeting is not as bad as what people make it out to be. You can have a lot of confidence that broad targeting can work well for you if you're passing back the right data through pixel conditioning."
[79:38]
"And in addition to that, if you are ensuring that you are being articulate towards who your target demographic is and the outcomes they want inside of all of the messaging throughout the entirety of your advertising process."
[79:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Creative Surplus - Your War Chest"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Conceito de "war chest": recursos abundantes esperando na lateral, não posição reativa de ter que filmar novo
[79:58]
"So back to the point, creative testing, I always want to have a surplus. Okay. The greatest like your war chest that helps keep you alive is a creative surplus."
[80:15]
"I want abundant resources just sitting on the sideline. Okay. I do not want to be in a position where I have to go and reactively film new stuff. That is the worst position to be in."
[80:30]
"Ad fatigue only gets worse once it happens. It does not get better. Okay? It gets worse."
[80:40]
"So, moral of the story is I have to have just a giant surplus of additional creatives just sitting there. That way, when fatigue occurs, I can swap them in."
[80:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Immediate Deployment Sin"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Erro comum visualizado: criar → imediatamente deployar = errado, criar → manter surplus → deployar quando necessário = correto
[80:58]
"The sin that I consistently see from advertisers is they just feel like as soon as they create something, they need to immediately deploy it. That's dumb. Makes no sense."
[81:15]
"It is better to create stuff so you remain ahead by just having it sit there on the sideline after it's created."
[81:28]
"Again, if I have campaigns that are performing well, the best time for me to test new creatives is when my current campaigns that are performing well and scaling fatigue."
[81:45]
"I don't need to launch a new campaign just for testing. Okay? I don't need another campaign targeting the exact same people, driving traffic to the exact same link that my campaigns that are scaling are going to just for testing random creatives. I do not need to do that."
[82:08]
"That creates audience overlap and artificially inflates my cost per result for both my scaling campaign and my test campaign."
[82:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What to Actually Test"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Foco correto de testes: outros funnels ou outras audiences, não creative testing em campanha duplicada
[82:25]
"What I want to test are other funnels or other audiences. Okay, I am testing creatives within one main campaign."
[82:38]
"If something works, great. Make a new version of it. And when ad fatigue occurs, pull out all of the previous images and videos. Put in the new version of what's previously worked and your new tests that are radically different from what worked."
[82:58]
"I do not need to artificially inflate my own costs and compete with myself with just creative testing. Okay? That is not an intended outcome of creative testing. Does that make sense to everybody?"
[83:15]
"It's like remember if something's not working, I'm going to relaunch the campaign anyway and I can put in new creatives. If something is working, I am scaling it."
[83:30]
"And when it stops, or I should say starts to give me indications that it's going to stop working, I'm relaunching it. And that's when I'm testing new stuff again."
[83:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Tests Are For Audiences and Funnels"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Clarificação: testes standalone = audiences e funnels, não creative testing que compete consigo mesmo
[83:48]
"My tests are for audience testing and funnel testing. Okay. And even then, rarely am I going to have a standalone campaign just testing a bunch of different audiences."
[84:05]
"In most instances, I'm going to wait until my current campaigns fatigues and then I'm going to relaunch it with some new creative testing and some audience testing if I want to do that, too."
[84:20]
"it's okay to do audience testing and creative testing, but ideally, okay, and this is very important you understand this. This is a very good pro tip."
[84:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "URL Duplication Pro Tip"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Hack avançado: duplicar funnel com URL diferente = plataforma pensa que é diferente = sem audience overlap
[84:35]
"Ideally, you duplicate the current funnel you have. You don't have to make a single change to it besides the URL. change the URL so it looks unique in the eyes of the platform that you're driving the traffic on and it will not create audience overlap for you."
[84:58]
"Because in the eyes of the platform, it does not understand what the fuck you're doing when you duplicate a campaign with the exact same audiences, the exact same place you're driving the traffic to."
[85:15]
"It's like, what the fuck is this guy doing? It has no idea why you're doing that. It doesn't make sense to it."
[85:25]
"If you're going to do that though, just do it with a different URL and then it thinks that that's a different URL. It thinks it's a different page even though it's the exact same content."
[85:40]
"And ideally, just to take it a step further than that, you do that in a backup ad account. That way, you condition that backup ad account and you further reduce the probability of any audience overlap occurring."
[85:58]
"There's a lot of best practices that occur for when you consider what can happen in the same account versus what opens up for you to be capable of doing without much negative consequence by just putting it into a different ad account. Does that make sense?"
[86:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A - Timothy's Audience Testing Question"
NÚMEROS: 3 tipos de cold audiences mencionados
VISUAL: Estrutura de audience testing: broad, lookalikes, giant interest stack - os 3 tipos principais
[86:20]
"Timothy here says, 'How much audience testing are you doing nowadays as opposed to just running broad?'"
[86:30]
"Yeah, I actually prefer to still do interest stacks. lookalikes are definitely doing well and then we'll also split test broad."
[86:45]
"So, our three different audiences that will typically run within a campaign, especially a scaled campaign, are if I'm doing cold, to be clear, let me be extremely direct on. If I'm doing cold targeting, okay, I want to test cold targeting."
[87:05]
"I'll do three different adsets. Broad lookalikes and a giant interest stack of who I think it's probable to do well with. So, the three different cold audience adsets that I would run and I would separate them."
[87:25]
"In addition to that, I would have a warm audience campaign, typically with just one ad set with all my warm audiences stacked together within that one ad set."
[87:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Back to Axel's Scaling Question"
NÚMEROS: 5x spend scaling mencionado
VISUAL: Retorno à pergunta sobre manter ROAS ao escalar 5x, resposta baseada em tudo já articulado sobre life cycle
[87:42]
"So, long story short, let's go back to some of the questions that relate to, as an example, like what Axel had asked earlier. Axel had specifically asked, 'How do you make sure ROAS doesn't go lower as you 5x the spend?'"
[88:00]
"Well, that answer should be very prevalent to you now based on everything I've articulated within the life cycle of a campaign."
[88:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Campaign Speed Visualization"
NÚMEROS: $200-$1000/day exemplos, 2 meses vs 3 semanas timelines
VISUAL: Desenho de curva de campanha: velocidade lenta = tempo longo, velocidade rápida (scaling) = tempo curto, gas pedal = budget
[88:12]
"Let me just make it really simple, right? I'm going to visualize it for you. So, it helps a little bit with retention."
[88:22]
"So, remember, if I visualize what a campaign does, I'm going to literally draw it at the speed in which we're going to go."
[88:35]
"So the first part of a campaign where it's probable to have the most resistance, the least probability for ROAS and then at the middle of the campaign, that's when we have the highest probability for performance and scale potential. And then at the end it can die."
[88:55]
"If I start it off, let's say like $1,000 a day or 200 a day or 500 a day, whatever it is, it's going to go slow. Okay? And then after I get through this window here, I'm like, 'All right, this is working really well. I'm going to scale.'"
[89:15]
"If I scale the fuck out of it really quick, I end up finishing the campaign really quick. Okay, if I maintain the same budget the entire time, I go at the same speed."
[89:30]
"if I speed up the budget as I scale, I speed up the process of going through the life cycle of a campaign. Okay, that's all I do."
[89:45]
"I just if I'm going to drive to a restaurant and I drive there at the same mile per hour the entire time, okay, versus obviously if I have stoplights along the way or I have the opportunity to just let it rip."
[90:00]
"All right, I'm getting there faster or I'm getting there slower relative to my speed. What determines speed in a campaign? What is the equivalent of a gas pedal in a campaign?"
[90:15]
"Somebody say it in the chat. What's the equivalent of us of a gas pedal? Spend and budget. That's exactly right."
[90:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Budget as Gas Pedal"
NÚMEROS: $500/day início → $10K/day fim possível, 2 meses ou 3 semanas variável
VISUAL: Metáfora completa: budget = gas pedal, aumentar budget = acelerar através do life cycle
[90:28]
"So, when I increase the budget to scale, okay, here's the thing. It's like this right here, as an example, might start off at like 500 a day and then this entire cycle of time right here, this window when I'm scaling up like I might be able to end the campaign at like $10,000 a day."
[90:50]
"And this entire window right here, this might end up being like two months. Okay? In other instances, it's like it might end up being like three weeks."
[91:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Audience Size Determines Campaign Distance"
NÚMEROS: Chris e Chad = 12K audience, $200/day = frequency 5 em 2 semanas; Real estate = $50K/day por mês possível
VISUAL: Comparação extrema: audience pequeno = fadiga rápida vs audience massivo = campanha longa, addressable market crítico
[91:08]
"One thing that also determines the distance that a campaign can go for, okay, the length of time that a campaign's probable to run for before we hit that plateau and then that decline, okay, is audience size and the quantity of people that are in market for what we are currently the advertising for and optimizing for."
[91:32]
"If I have a tight small audience, okay, I'll give you a good example. There's two inner circle members, Chris and Chad. Chris and Chad have a target audience of about 12,000 people on Facebook and Instagram."
[91:50]
"They could spend $200 a day and be at a frequency of like five on the campaign within two weeks. How long is a campaign probable to spend before it hits a plateau and an eventual fatigue for them?"
[92:08]
"Comparatively, if I run to cold audiences and let's say like as an example, I always love the real estate info product offers as a great example of this because there's so many people on earth that actively believe that real estate creates millionaires."
[92:30]
"So many people that have that thought in their head and there's also so many people that both are in it already and want to get in it that you could spend $50,000 a day."
[92:45]
"And no exaggeration, that campaign might have life for a month before it dies. If Chris and Chad with an audience of 12,000 people spent $50,000 a day, it would fatigue within the day."
[93:05]
"So the distance a campaign can go before I start to experience ROAS is determined by audience size and addressable market. Okay. Comparatively bigger audiences mean greater lengths of time."
[93:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Maintaining ROAS While Scaling - Summary"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Lista completa de fatores: self-awareness no ciclo, creative surplus pronto, identificar fadiga cedo, superar rapidamente
[93:28]
"So again I have to ensure like how do I ensure that I have a great ROAS as I scale self-awareness on where I'm at in the advertising cycle."
[93:42]
"a creative surplus sitting there ready to test with for when ad fatigue occurs. Being able to identify that ad fatigue is about to occur or is occurring and overcoming it that determines whether ROAS maintains itself as I scale."
[94:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Beyond Advertising - People Variables"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Fatores além de ads: show rate, close rate, AOV, morale de sales team, lead quality, separação marketing vs people
[94:02]
"In addition to that, there's some things outside of our control as advertisers in different campaigns. Let's say I'm running traffic to a sales team and the show rate all of a sudden tanks or the close rate tanks or the AOV tanks. That'll affect my ROAS as well."
[94:22]
"So there's marketing knobs and then there's people. Okay, I've got to maintain the statistics as I scale as well. I don't want to be negligent to that. I want to make sure you're very aware of that."
[94:38]
"I have to maintain statistics as I scale. If the statistics flux against me, my ROAS will flux against me. And sometimes that is beyond the life cycle of a campaign. And it doesn't have anything to do with fatigue."
[94:58]
"It has to do with your close rates the bed because your sales people have got low morale all of a sudden because all of a sudden lead quality change, you know, because all of a sudden blah blah blah. You see what I mean? You got to keep all that in mind, too."

[95:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Chadvertising Summary - ROAS is Holy Grail"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Resumo final: ROAS = stat supremo, bottlenecks só importam quando ROAS ruim, foco em escala sempre
[95:50]
"So moral of the story when it comes to creative testing and ad optimization. Okay, I want to make this really clear just to reiterate. We are attempting to chadvertise."
[96:08]
"Chad advertising for us means that the holy grail, okay, the ultimate stat, okay, is ROAS that determines what we do more than anything else."
[96:22]
"From there, we can look at the sub-stats and try to identify bottlenecks. Okay? So, when things are not working, we're trying to find the choke point."
[96:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Bottleneck Categories"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Lista exaustiva de possíveis bottlenecks: CPMs, CTR, conversion rate, watch rate BSL, application drop-off, show rate, close rate, AOV, etc.
[96:38]
"choke points could be CPMs, link click-through rates, conversion rates on the page, on the funnel, things like sub-stats even of that like watch rates on BSLs, application drop off rates, you know, things like this."
[96:58]
"If it's call funnels or webinars or challenge funnels, you know, show rates, close rates. If we have two call closes, we have the second call booking rate, the second call show rate. We have things like AOV and each one of these have sub-stats that can make up that individual stat."
[97:20]
"But again these statistics that are in the bottleneck category, when do they count? When do they matter? They matter when ROAS is not where we want it to be."
[97:35]
"as I mentioned, there is no static answer for any one of these particular statistics. There's good benchmarks to be aware of, but again, all of these statistics are dynamic."
[97:50]
"We use Devon as an example for saying, okay, you have a 7 to 1 ROAS, but your CPMs are high and your link clickthrough rate is 0.5%. Does it matter? No. Because again, ROAS trumps everything in that example."
[98:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The TL;DR - Too Long Didn't Read"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Resumo ultra-simplificado: funciona + bom ROAS = ESCALA; não funciona + ROAS ruim = identifica bottleneck e corrige
[98:12]
"if I'm scaling and one of my campaigns or my main campaign, my ROAS starts to drop, what am I going to do? I'm going to go through and I'm going to identify the bottleneck."
[98:28]
"the ultimate thing to consider the summary like the too long didn't read version of all this is, okay, if it's working and you got good ROAS, scale it."
[98:42]
"If it's not working and your ROAS is not where you want it to be, look at where the bottleneck is and actively pay attention to it more than anything else. Overcome the bottleneck. That's the moral of the story."
[98:58]
"Does that make sense to everybody? Very good. Questions? Feel free to unmute yourself, drop them into the chat. Let's get these things answered and let's wrap it up. What do you guys got?"
[99:15]
"When do you start considering split testing? Uh, I'm appalled at that question. I must have done an absolutely terrible job during this presentation for you to ask that question."
[99:30]
"I am split testing literally the entire time as I just described. Maybe I just need to say it in more simple English. Uh, let me reiterate it assuming that it's my fault that you asked that question."

[104:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Bottleneck-Based Decision Making"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Framework de decisão: identificar bottleneck → atacar o que é mais fácil de dobrar ou reduzir pela metade
[104:28]
"Great question. Thank you for unmuting yourself. Long story short, it's all based on where the bottleneck is in the process in that instance."
[104:42]
"So, just to be a little more articulate, if I'm driving traffic to a VSSL, short form or long form, and I'm getting good feedback from the sales team that like leads are coming through well framed and like, you know, people the sales team's liking lead quality."
[105:02]
"But maybe I have too high of a cost per result. My attention likely won't go to the video in that case. You know, my attention would likely go to, as an example, again, just wherever the bottleneck is."
[105:20]
"If my link click-through rate is low and I want to drive down my cost, I could double that statistic if it's the easiest to double. You're trying to play the doubles game with bottlenecks or technically the reduction in half game, depending on the statistic."
[105:42]
"If I have a really shitty link click-through rate, I could obviously pay a lot of attention to that if that's what's easiest to double and it would obviously reduce my cost in head."
[105:58]
"If my like application rate on the page is dog shit and that's easiest to double, you know, maybe by changing a question or removing a question or just reframing how people are going through the application."
[106:15]
"The point being, it's like whatever's easiest to double or easiest to reduce in half, that's where I'm going to put my attention. It's not again it's not a very static answer. It's wherever needs the attention to get the outcome that we're looking for. That's the moral of the story. Does that make sense?"
[106:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Call Funnel Unqualified Leads Example"
NÚMEROS: $70K liquid capital requirement adicionado
VISUAL: Caso específico: leads não qualificados = problema nos ADS não no vídeo, solução = mudar messaging em ads
[106:40]
"So like when I was getting the the unqualified people in the call funnel example for that new client I articulated to you guys. I didn't look at it like oh my video sucks and that's why I'm getting unqualified people."
[107:00]
"I looked at it like my ads are attracting unqualified people so I'm going to change the messaging in the ads and that's going to give me the outcome I'm looking for in this case."
[107:15]
"So again, I just restarted every single ad with if you have this amount of money or access to this amount of money then and then then we said what we normally say in the other ads."
[107:32]
"Makes sense."
[107:35]
"Yep. Thank you."

[108:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Final Q&A - Budget and Adset Structure"
NÚMEROS: $1,000 ou menos, cold audiences, interesse histórico funcionou
VISUAL: Pergunta sobre número de adsets com budget baixo, resposta: menos variáveis = melhor
[108:24]
"So when you were doing the maths for $1,000 or less for the campaign, it was three to five images or videos, two to five headlines, two to three body copy, but how many adsets usually if it's less than $1,000?"
[108:42]
"As few as I can have. Yeah, as few as I can have. Remember like the fewer variables the better at smaller budgets."
[108:55]
"So, are you doing warm audiences in that example or cold audiences?"
[109:00]
"Cold audiences."
[109:02]
"Have you had any history in the account of a particular cold audience type working best?"
[109:10]
"for a different offer? Yes. But kind of similar, but for this one, it's like a completely new offer."
[109:20]
"So, there's three different types of cold audiences. There's interest stack targeting which also includes demographic and behavior traits. Lookalike audiences or broad. Okay. Have you had any singular performance with one of those three that have been better than the others in the past?"
[109:42]
"Interest."
[109:44]
"Yeah. Great. So, I'd likely start with that. I would start with a cold campaign, a singular ad set with just a big old interest stack in it."
[110:00]
"And then I would do my creative testing that we talked about there with two to three different images or or videos, two to three different headlines, two to three different pieces of body copy. Smaller the variables, the higher the probability of performance."
[110:20]
"Would it still be valid to have like two, three different adsets that have slightly different interest stacks?"
[110:30]
"Absolutely not. That's dumb. Put them all inside of one giant adset. Makes no sense to separate them out."
[110:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Interest Stacking Strategy"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Estratégia correta: todos os interesses em 1 adset gigante vs separar em múltiplos adsets (errado), evitar overlap
[110:45]
"there's likely excessive overlap between all of them in that example. Well, the only real reason that most advertisers do that is they're attempting to determine like which of all the interests they have are the ones that are going to be the ones getting the results."
[111:05]
"When in reality, if you put them all within one specific ad set and you just do a big old stack of them, you're just combining everybody that's probable to convert anyway into a bigger audience."
[111:20]
"So you're giving yourself more scale potential and you're giving yourself a bigger audience of people initially that are probable to convert all out of one ad set which is better in comparison to splitting them off. Does that make sense?"
[111:38]
"So for example, yeah, it makes sense. So for example, I could take the the Jeremy Well stack that is posted for example, add all kinds of trading interest to it on top of it."
[111:55]
"And just run that."
[111:58]
"Yep."
[112:00]
"Exactly. That that's exactly right. Correct. And that's a lot smarter. Like I said, it's an account simplification best practice that you're following when you do that."
[112:15]
"You're increasing your probability of performance from that ad set rather than decreasing it by increasing the size. Does that make sense?"
[112:28]
"Makes sense. And also, you would just have the on the ad level one ad with all of the creatives as dynamic. Correct."
[112:40]
"I would I would personally do dynamic creative. However, it's okay if you do static ads. just don't create too many individual static variations, you know, kind of keep it simpler."
[112:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Dynamic vs Static Ads - Current Glitch"
NÚMEROS: 3 meses de glitch mencionado
VISUAL: Problema técnico atual: dynamic virando flexible, soluções: export CSV, custom column, ou reverter para static
[113:00]
"The thing that I've been facing with dynamic for now because I've just gone back to running ads myself that occupied my media buyer. So, it's like how do you like at the result level when you're trying to see results, it doesn't tell you exactly which one got me the result. For example,"
[113:22]
"there's a glitch right now across the campaigns where it's like they're switching from dynamic to flexible ads. And that glitch has been around now for about three months. There's been a few ways to resolve it, but it doesn't always work to be clear."
[113:42]
"Way number one is to export the data. So like there's a little button in the top right to export to a CSV or Excel sheet that can show it."
[113:55]
"Two, if you create a custom column, the custom column can literally be as simple as pulling any other statistic that's not from a standard default column. That's gotten it to show"
[114:12]
"or in some instances if it still doesn't show, then just revert back to static creative. It's fine either way. Again, I don't care if you do dynamic creative or not. just been a great best practice for us historically. But we also typically run at higher budgets."
[114:32]
"So if you're a smaller budget, it's still good to control the spend for like where it goes. And if and if like I said, if you run at 500 a day and you do dynamic ads and you can't see what's working and what's not, then again, if your account doesn't resolve with those other two solutions I just proposed, then switch back to static is cool."
[114:58]
"All right. Thanks. Makes sense."
[115:02]
"Cool. Sweet. Other questions? Anybody?"
[115:10]
"All right, sweet. All right, cool. Appreciate it. Edward, by the way, your advertiser was trying to join into the group in the call. We didn't know he was your advertiser, so we denied him from both, but we'll let him in now. I'm sorry."
[115:28]
"All right, cool. Talk soon, everybody. Bye."
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
Devon's Campaign: $130 CPM, 0.5% CTR, 7x ROAS
Timothy's Campaign: 2x ROAS front-end, $80K/mês spend = $160K revenue, continuidade mensal perpétua
Cody Sanchez Challenge: $292K spend, 1.9x front-end ROAS, $3.4M back-end cash, $10K produto, 12% upsell para $35K (~300 compradores)
Dan Martell Account: $1.6M/mês, modelo liquidação front-end
Google Adwords Personal Injury: $200-$500 por CLIQUE
Campaign Lifespan: $35K-$50K/dia = 5-7 dias fadiga; $1K-$5K/dia = 3-9 semanas
Chris & Chad Audience: 12K pessoas apenas
Budget Testing Variables: ≤$1K = 3-5 imagens/vídeos, 2-3 headlines, 2-3 body copy
Dynamic Creative Math: 10 vídeos × 5 headlines × 5 body copy = 250 combinações
Brandon Carter Filming: 5 camisetas, 10 locações, 3-4 horas = dezenas de vídeos
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Chadvertising Philosophy:
ROAS é a métrica suprema que supera todas as outras
Não existem respostas estáticas em advertising - tudo é dinâmico
Foco constante em ESCALA, não em otimização prematura
"Strike while the iron's hot" - escalar quando lucrativo
Front-End vs Back-End:
Atenção igual necessária em ambos
30-day ROAS vs lifetime value
Modelo de ascensão low ticket → high ticket
Backend pode transformar sub-1x ROAS em 12x ROAS total
Campaign Life Cycle:
Início: Baixíssima probabilidade ROAS, julgar por "signs of life"
Meio: Máxima probabilidade ROAS, momento de escala agressiva
Fim: Plateau e morte inevitável (ad fatigue)
Signs of Life (Leading Indicators):
Stats trending na direção correta
Feedback positivo de sales team
Lead quality melhorando
Custo por resultado caindo/estabilizando ("bottoming out")
Test Windows:
3-10 dias para novas campanhas (variável por contexto)
Budget de teste = quanto você está disposto a PERDER
Timetables apertam janela (webinars vs call funnels)
Data Analysis Windows:
Diário: apenas check, nunca decisões
3 dias: indicação de tendência, decisões ocasionais
7 dias: confirmação forte de tendência
Month-to-date: visão favorita para conclusões finais
Creative Testing:
Manter SURPLUS massivo sempre
Nunca testar imediatamente após criar
Testar apenas quando: 1) Ad fatigue, 2) ROAS ruim, 3) Início de campanha
Budget determina quantidade de variáveis
Baixo budget = MENOS variáveis (plataforma vai concentrar de qualquer forma)
Detachment Mastery:
Nunca se apegar a campanhas ou creatives
Recriar, não reusar
Plataformas odeiam repetição
Fadiga = oportunidade de teste
Overcoming Ad Fatigue:
Trocar APENAS imagens/vídeos (headlines e body copy são secundários)
Fazer "novo": mudar roupa, local, adicionar/remover B-roll, usar chapéu/óculos
Mesmas palavras em contexto visual diferente = novo para plataforma
Audience Testing Structure:
3 adsets cold: Broad, Lookalikes, Giant Interest Stack
1 adset warm: todos warm audiences juntos
NUNCA separar interesses similares (causa overlap)
Stack TUDO em um adset gigante
Scaling Strategy:
Budget = gas pedal (determina velocidade através do life cycle)
Audience size determina "distância" possível
Addressable market crítico para longevidade
Inflationary events (Super Bowl, Valentine's, etc.) aceleram fadiga
ESTRUTURA:
Bottleneck Identification:
CPMs, CTR, Conversion Rate, Watch Rate, Application Rate, Show Rate, Close Rate, AOV
Atacar o que é mais fácil de DOBRAR ou REDUZIR PELA METADE
Bottlenecks só importam quando ROAS está ruim
Account Simplification:
Menos campanhas = melhor
Concentrar budget para testar mais variáveis
URL duplication hack: mesmo funnel, URL diferente = sem overlap
Backup ad accounts para testes sem interferência
The Doubles Game:
Identificar stat mais fácil de dobrar
Ou mais fácil de reduzir pela metade
Depende do bottleneck específico
TL;DR (Too Long Didn't Read):
Funciona + bom ROAS = ESCALA
Não funciona + ROAS ruim = identifica bottleneck → corrige
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Exemplo scaling: $80K → $500K spend = diferença de $420K lucro (2x ROAS mantido)
Mark Jennison: massive ROAS via back-end focus
Real estate offers: $50K/dia sustentável por mês em cold traffic
Personal injury lawyers: $200-$500 CPC justificável por ROAS
Challenge model: investimento $292K → retorno $4M+ (30 dias + upsells)
DURAÇÃO: ~115 minutos
FORMATO: Weekly group call / masterclass sobre ad optimization e creative testing
